attributes.stem: A term newborn presents with signs of respiratory distress after birth and is transferred to the neonatal intensive care unit. Clinical examination reveals pallor, cyanosis of the lips and tongue, weak peripheral pulses, cold extremities, rapid breathing with flared nostrils, and bobbing of the head. Auscultation of the chest reveals a prominent S2. Urgent echocardiography confirms the diagnosis of hypoplastic left heart syndrome. Immediate initiation of continuous prostaglandin E1 infusion occurs, followed by the Norwood procedure. Nine days after the surgery, an ECG shows the following abnormality.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate definitive management?
attributes.explanation:
This ECG shows features characteristic of a complete heart block. Arrhythmias are a common complication after the Norwood procedure. Undergoing concomitant procedures, particularly involving the tricuspid valve, have been associated with atrioventricular (AV) block. AV block also may be associated with increased intermediate-term mortality. In one-third of cases of complete heart block post-operative for congenital heart disease surgery, AV conduction does not recover and these patients should undergo pacemaker implantation. High-grade AV block following congenital heart disease surgery that exceeds 7-10 days, even in a setting of a narrow QRS escape rate, warrants permanent pacemaker implantation. As an immediate intervention, temporary pacing wires can be placed to maintain chronotropy. Indications for permanent pacing in those with acquired heart disease are very similar to those with congenital heart disease. Generally, all symptomatic non-reversible AV nodal disease will require permanent pacemaker implantation.

IV adrenaline can be utilised for the acute management of this patient, however, it is not a definitive management. It can be used in children with bradycardia and poor perfusion or shock.

IV atropine can be utilised for the acute management of this patient, however, it is not a definitive management. It is recommended for bradycardia in children caused by an increased vagal tone or a primary AV block. In this particular case, the heart block is likely a consequence of the surgical procedure rather than vagal overactivity or primary AV block, rendering atropine less efficacious as an acute or definitive treatment modality.

After the Norwood procedure, aspirin can be administrated to prevent shunt thrombosis. While it holds significance in postoperative care, it does not directly treat the complete AV block.

Percutaneous coronary intervention is not the most appropriate definitive management for complete heart block. This procedure is primarily used as a treatment in acute coronary syndrome. It is not directly applicable to the management of heart block, especially in a neonate with hypoplastic left heart syndrome.

Hypoplastic left heart syndrome (HLHS) is a type of cyanotic congenital heart defect characterised by abnormal development of left-sided structures of the heart. This generally includes underdevelopment of the left ventricle resulting in a hypoplastic or absent left ventricle, and also possibly stenosis and/or atresia of the mitral valve, aortic valve and/or aortic arch. The result is a non-functional left ventricle and obstruction to blood flow from the left ventricular tract. This means the systemic and pulmonary circulation is maintained primarily by the right ventricle. An atrial septal defect allows for the passage of oxygenated blood from the left atrium to the right atrium and a patent ductus arteriosus allows for blood flow from the pulmonary artery to the aorta, which reaches the systemic circulation.

The surgical management of hypoplastic left heart syndrome (HLHS) comprises three essential components vital for patient survival. The primary objective is to separate the systemic and pulmonary circulations. The initial stage involves the Norwood procedure, typically conducted within the first week of life. Its purpose is to utilise the right ventricle as the pumping chamber for the aorta. This is achieved through the reconstruction of the aortic arch and its connection to the right ventricle. This entails separating the main pulmonary artery from its right and left branches and joining it to the upper segment of the aorta. Additionally, a systemic to pulmonary artery shunt is established to facilitate blood flow to the pulmonary arteries. Shunt options include the modified Blalock-Thomas-Taussig shunt (mBTTs), connecting the innominate artery to the pulmonary arteries, a central shunt from the ascending aorta to the pulmonary arteries, or a Sano shunt connecting the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery.

The second stage procedure, known as the bidirectional Glenn operation, is typically performed when the patient reaches four to six months of age. This operation involves establishing an anastomosis between the superior vena cava and the right pulmonary artery, allowing venous return to directly flow into both lungs. This redirection of blood flow reduces preload on the right ventricle, preventing right ventricular hypertrophy and promoting improved diastolic function.

The third and final stage encompasses the Fontan operation, typically performed between the ages of two and five years. This procedure entails directing blood flow from the inferior vena cava to the pulmonary arteries via either a lateral tunnel procedure or an extracardiac conduit. These approaches serve to optimise circulation by channelling blood directly to the pulmonary arteries, bypassing the right ventricle.

HLHS is a uniformly fatal disease if not treated surgically. This disease usually presents during the first week of life with symptoms of low systemic perfusion following closure of the patent ductus arteriosus (from the decreased pulmonary vascular resistance) which manifests as cyanosis (not corrected by supplemental oxygen), hypotension, hypoxaemia, tachycardia, tachypnoea, and cardiogenic shock. Detection is possible on prenatal ultrasound of the foetal heart between 18 and 22 weeks gestation. Second-trimester foetal echocardiography can reveal a small, abnormally functioning left ventricle, and aortic stenosis and/or coarctation of the aorta. Echocardiography is a confirmatory test and shows a hypoplastic or absent left ventricle. HLHS more commonly occurs in males. Genetic disorders associated with HLHS include Turner syndrome, Holt-Oram syndrome, Smith Lemli Opitz syndrome, Noonan syndrome, and trisomy 13, 18, 21.
learningPoints: Consider permanent pacing in those with high-grade AV blocks post-operatively after congenital heart disease surgery that exceeds 7-10 days. Temporary pacing wires can be utilised to maintain adequate chronotropy.
references: Ferreira CR, Aiello VD, de Melo AMAGP, et al. Classic form of hypoplastic left heart syndrome diagnosed post-natally: an autopsy report. Autops Case Rep. 2014 Sep 30;4(3):21-30. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5444395/

Oster ME, Chen S, Dagincourt N, et al. Development and impact of arrhythmias after the Norwood procedure: A report from the Pediatric Heart Network. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg. 2017 Mar;153(3):638-645.e2. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27939495/

Rao, S. (2021). Pediatric Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome: Background, Pathophysiology, Etiology. [online] Medscape.com. Available at: https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/890196-overview?icd=login_success_email_match_norm#a4 [Accessed 9 Jun. 2023].

Sadineni RT, Kumar BS, Chander NB, Boppana DM. Prenatal Sonographic Diagnosis of Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome. Int J Appl Basic Med Res. 2017 Jul-Sep;7(3):213-215. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5590391/

Shrikhande, D.Y., Chatterjee, R., Nilesh Maniya and Kumar, A. (2014). Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome –A Rare Case Report. [online] ResearchGate. Available at: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/315013896_Hypoplastic_Left_Heart_Syndrome_-A_Rare_Case_Report [Accessed 9 Jun. 2023].
